title: He Kākano Ahau: I Am a Seed - Understanding Our Place in the Mangakōtukutuku Stream Community
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore the significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream) as a living ancestor and community connector. Drawing from Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau (stories) about the stream's formation and its role in sustaining local hapū, learners will investigate how this waterway continues to shape community identity, relationships, and responsibilities in modern Hastings.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain at least three ways Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku connects people, places, and practices within our local community
Conduct respectful interviews with local kaumātua and community members to gather perspectives on stream guardianship
Create a collaborative digital storymap documenting the cultural and ecological significance of specific sites along the awa
Propose and justify one actionable community initiative that demonstrates kaitiakitanga of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa: Story Gathering
activities.description: Students will visit significant sites along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua to hear pūrākau about the stream's creation and historical significance. Using audio recorders, they will document stories and create annotated sketches of cultural landmarks.

activities.title: Ngā Kōrero a te Kāinga: Community Voice Collection
activities.description: Working in pairs, students will develop interview questions and conduct recorded interviews with diverse community members (farmers, local iwi representatives, council members, residents) about their relationship with the awa and visions for its future.

activities.title: He Whenua He Tangata: Digital Storymapping
activities.description: Using ArcGIS StoryMaps, students will create interactive digital maps integrating historical photos, interview excerpts, cultural significance markers, and current environmental data points along the stream's pathway.

activities.title: Te Hononga: Connecting Past to Present
activities.description: Students will analyze how traditional practices of resource gathering and management along the awa can inform modern environmental challenges, comparing historical uses with current water quality data and urban development impacts.

activities.title: He Rautaki Mō Āpōpō: Action Planning
activities.description: Groups will develop and present specific proposals for community action (e.g., planting days, water monitoring program, cultural interpretation signs) that address both cultural preservation and environmental sustainability of the awa.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and knowledge holders from local marae
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' water quality data for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
ArcGIS StoryMaps platform and school iPads with recording capabilities
'Te Awa Atua: The River of Life' by Anne Salmond (specific chapters on waterway significance)

tasks: Digital storymap presentation demonstrating understanding of cultural and ecological connections
Written reflection on interview findings and personal positionality regarding kaitiakitanga
Group proposal for community action with justification based on research and cultural principles

nzcAlignment: Identity, Culture, and Organization: Students explore how communities pass on and sustain culture
Place and Environment: Understand how people interact with natural and cultural environments
Continuity and Change: Understand how past events, experiences, and actions influence the present
The Economic World: Understand how people manage economic resources and respond to challenges
